title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The history of Kelvin Hardy and his sister, Helen, is recounted, noting that after Helen disappeared, Dr. Kennedy searched the house and found her packed suitcase and clothes, but no body. Following his belief that he had hallucinated strangling her, Kelvin entered a private sanatorium in Norfolk for treatment, where he died two years later. After her disappearance, Helen sent two letters: one from Biarritz, France, stating she was going to Spain, and a second six months later from Florence, addressed to "Miss Kennedy," stating she did not want a divorce but would not grant one without proof. Kelvin chose not to divorce her. Gwendolyn, searching for news of Helen, reveals she heard her father confess to strangling her.

Jules and Gwendolyn discuss the inconsistencies in the case. Gwendolyn points out that she saw a body in the foyer, whereas Kelvin told Dr. Kennedy he strangled Helen in the bedroom. Jules argues that if Kelvin had actually killed and buried Helen, he would not have confessed the crime to Dr. Kennedy, a man with whom he was not close. Jules also notes that the absence of a death record in the parish register proves Helen did not die of natural causes. He suggests the letters might be forgeries designed to mimic Helen's handwriting to reinforce Kelvin's belief that she had run away. Jules concludes that the pattern of a staged departure—including the packed clothes and timed letters—is typical of a calculated murderer, though this contradicts the behavior of a passionate killer who would confess impulsively. Jules posits that an unknown factor, "X," is involved in the orchestration of these events.
